does: This paper provides a comprehensive taxonomy and detailed analysis of mechanistic interpretability approaches for neural networks. It systematically categorizes reverse-engineering techniques aimed at uncovering and translating the internal computational algorithms of deep neural networks into human-understandable forms. The work goes beyond surface-level explanations by focusing on the actual mechanisms and algorithms that networks implement internally, offering concrete examples and pseudo-code implementations of key MI techniques. The paper positions mechanistic interpretability as a distinctive research program within the broader explainable AI landscape, establishing a structured framework for understanding how different MI methods relate to and differ from each other.
connects: This work builds on decades of research in neural network interpretability and explainability, synthesizing insights from feature visualization, attention analysis, probing methods, and circuit-based analysis. It extends prior efforts to make neural networks transparent, responding to limitations of earlier post-hoc explanation methods that lack mechanistic grounding. The paper connects to foundational work in understanding learned representations, building on progress in identifying circuits and interpretable substructures within networks. It also relates to broader XAI traditions including LIME, SHAP, and gradient-based methods, while arguing that MI provides deeper causal understanding of network computation compared to these alternative approaches.
enables: This unified taxonomy and analytical framework enables future research to build more systematic mechanistic interpretability tools by clarifying which techniques address which interpretability questions. It provides a foundation for developing more sophisticated reverse-engineering methods that could scale to larger models and more complex behaviors. The work enables practitioners to deploy AI systems with greater transparency guarantees by establishing clearer links between network internals and observable behaviors. It points toward future capabilities in understanding emergent behaviors, identifying failure modes proactively, and potentially steering or controlling neural network computations at the mechanistic level, supporting the development of more trustworthy and auditable AI systems.
